explanation:
**Normal health values** come from massive population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. They're guidelines; your personal medical history always takes priority.

## How It's Calculated

Subcutaneous fat lies beneath your skin. Visceral fat surrounds your organs—the more dangerous type.

This calculator applies the formula using your input values and checks them against typical ranges, alerting you if values fall outside normal limits.

## Normal Reference Ranges

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60–100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1–37.2°C |
| Blood Oxygen (SpO₂) | 95–100% |
| Fasting Glucose | 70–100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL Cholesterol | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL Cholesterol | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What's the difference between subcutaneous and visceral fat?
faq.a: Subcutaneous fat lies under your skin and is pinchable. Visceral fat surrounds your organs deep in the abdomen, making it metabolically active and linked to greater health risks.

faq.q: Why is visceral fat more dangerous than subcutaneous fat?
faq.a: Visceral fat is metabolically active and linked to inflammation, insulin resistance, and increased risk of heart disease, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome—even in people with normal weight.
It uses internationally validated formulas. Results are estimates for reference only. For medical diagnosis, consult a healthcare professional.

faq.q: Can men and women use the same formula?
faq.a: Formulas account for physiological differences between men and women where applicable for accurate body composition estimates.
